does: This paper develops an adversarial attack against LLM-powered reverse engineering agents like GhidraMCP that automate binary analysis. The core contribution is a genetic algorithm-based prompt injection technique, adapted from AutoDAN, that crafts adversarial prompts designed to fool LLMs into misinterpreting decompiled code. The method embeds malicious instructions within string variable assignments in decompiled binaries, causing the LLM agent to produce corrupted or misleading analysis output. The authors demonstrate this proof-of-concept attack successfully deceives state-of-the-art LLMs processing machine code, showing that agentic reverse engineering systems are vulnerable to prompt injection attacks that degrade their analytical reliability.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in LLM-powered software analysis tools and agentic systems that combine LLMs with specialized instruments like Ghidra for automated reverse engineering. It directly extends research on adversarial attacks against LLMs, particularly prompt injection and AutoDAN-style optimization-based prompt generation. The paper responds to the emerging vulnerability space created by integrating powerful language models into security-critical domains like malware analysis, where LLMs have shown promise but lack robustness guarantees. It connects to broader concerns about LLM reliability in code understanding tasks and the tension between automation benefits and new attack surfaces in cybersecurity workflows.
enables: This work opens directions for developing defenses against prompt injection attacks in code analysis contexts, including input sanitization strategies and adversarially robust LLM architectures for processing potentially hostile decompiled binaries. It motivates research into formal verification of LLM behavior on machine code interpretation and methods for detecting when LLMs have been compromised via prompt attacks. The findings could drive development of detection mechanisms that identify maliciously crafted binaries designed to exploit LLMs, as well as hybrid human-AI workflows that maintain security analyst involvement. More broadly, it points toward the need for adversarial robustness testing frameworks specifically designed for agentic systems in cybersecurity applications before widespread deployment.
